summary: Cantrill continues cataloging the flood of blog posts from OpenSolaris's Opening Day launch, organizing them into categories including system calls, build process and workspace management, and debuggability. He highlights how Sun engineers are using blogs to document previously unwritten tribal knowledge about Solaris internals. The post emphasizes the Solaris team's obsession with debuggability and reliability, noting they built custom tools like mdb and DTrace because existing tools were insufficient for their needs.
key_points: Blogs serve as the most scalable way to transfer knowledge from those who have it to those who need it
The Solaris team requires lint-clean code and takes build process integrity extremely seriously
Debuggability is central to Solaris development ethos - the OS must be reliable above all else
The team built custom debugging tools (mdb, DTrace) because existing tools couldn't meet their needs
Early workspace management tools were 'all blade and no handle' - useful but dangerous
money_quotes: Blogs have become a critical conduit of information for us – we believe that they are the most scalable way to get information from the people who have it to the people who need it.
This is an accurate reflection of our ethos in developing Solaris: the operating system must be reliable above all else, and we view debugging the operating system as our primary responsibility.
This responsibility runs deeper than just the act of debugging, because our needs so outstripped existing tools that we designed and built our own.

For a long time, wx was a shell script in Bonwick's home directory. It was incredibly useful, but it was also easy to accidentally blow your brains out. As Bart is fond of saying, it was 'all blade and no handle.'
We require the Solaris source to be lint-clean.
key_insight: Building reliable systems software requires not just debugging skills but also building custom debugging tools and designing subsystems with debuggability as a first-class concern.
